title: Disinfect Water with Bleach
summary: Use plain unscented household bleach of known strength to disinfect relatively clear water, then wait at least 30 minutes before drinking. This is a backup method for when boiling is not possible.
quickAnswer: Add the correct dose of plain unscented household bleach to clear water, stir, and wait at least 30 minutes. Do not use scented, splashless, or color-safe bleach.
whenToUse: Boiling is not possible and you have plain unscented household bleach of known concentration
Bottled or boiled water is unavailable and you must disinfect water from a tap, well, or relatively clear surface source
preferredAction: Use plain unscented household bleach with a known sodium hypochlorite percentage. Add the correct emergency dose for your bleach concentration and water volume, stir, and wait at least 30 minutes before drinking.
backupAction: If bleach concentration is unknown or the label is missing, do not guess — find a different water source or treatment method. If bleach is unavailable entirely, use approved water purification tablets or boil the water.
stepByStepActions: Confirm the bleach is plain, unscented, and not splashless, color-safe, or additive-containing. Check the label for the sodium hypochlorite percentage — correct dosing depends on concentration.
If the water is cloudy, let it settle for several minutes, then pour through a clean cloth, paper towel, or coffee filter. Bleach is significantly less effective in cloudy water.
Add the correct emergency dose for your bleach concentration and the volume of water you are treating. Use official dosage guidance from EPA or CDC — do not estimate.
Stir or shake the water well, then let it stand for at least 30 minutes before drinking. If the water is cold (below 12°C / 55°F) or still slightly cloudy, wait at least 60 minutes.
After the wait time, the water should have a faint but detectable chlorine smell. If you cannot smell any chlorine, add another dose and wait the same amount of time again.
If the water still has no chlorine smell after a second dose, do not drink it — the water may be too contaminated for bleach treatment to work. Find an alternative source.
Store treated water in clean, covered containers. Label them clearly as treated water with the date.
warnings: Using the wrong bleach type or the wrong dose can be unsafe. Wrong-type bleach may introduce toxic additives; overdosing creates a hazardous solution.
Bleach does not remove chemical contaminants, fuel, heavy metals, or pesticides. Do not use this method if the water may have chemical or fuel contamination.
Bleach is significantly less effective in cold water (below 12°C / 55°F) or in turbid (cloudy) water. Extend wait time or find a cleaner source.
whatNotToDo: Do not use scented, splashless, color-safe, or additive-containing bleach — only plain unscented household bleach with a labeled sodium hypochlorite percentage.
Do not guess at the bleach concentration if the label is missing or unreadable — unknown concentration makes correct dosing impossible.
Do not mix bleach with other cleaners, chemicals, or ammonia-based products.
Do not drink water that smells overwhelmingly of chlorine or has a burning quality — this indicates too much bleach was used.
redFlags: Burning in the mouth or throat, breathing difficulty, or severe pain after bleach exposure — indicates concentrated bleach ingestion, a medical emergency. Call emergency services.
Persistent vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, or fever appearing after drinking treated water — signs of waterborne illness or chemical exposure.
Bleach container is unlabeled, label is unreadable, or concentration is unknown — do not use this bleach for water treatment.
preparednessTips: Keep a small bottle of plain unscented liquid bleach (5–8% sodium hypochlorite) in your emergency kit and replace it every 6–12 months as bleach degrades.
Store a printed EPA or CDC emergency water disinfection dosage table covering common container sizes (1 quart, 1 gallon, 5 gallons) and bleach concentrations (6%, 8.25%).
Make sure all adult household members know how to identify correct bleach, measure a dose, and perform the chlorine smell test.

notes: Consolidated from purify-water-bleach (canonical) and purifying-water-with-household-liquid-bleach (archived). Steps: 7 final steps (best-of merge from 4 canonical + 6 archive). whatNotToDo: 4 items (archive superset absorbed canonical). preparednessTips: 3 items from archive (canonical had none). Source refs from canonical (real URLs). One conflict resolved: wait time standardized to 30 min (clear/warm) / 60 min (cold/cloudy) per EPA guidance. Archive slug 'purifying-water-with-household-liquid-bleach' should be set to review_status:archived by a human AFTER this version is approved and released.
